Tafseer:
طسم تلک ایت الکتب المبین (2-1)
Discussions on the disjointed letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at) have passed previously. The aspects of favor, blessing, and the establishment of ultimate proof (itmaam al-hujjah) inherent in 'the clear Book' (al-Kitab al-Mubeen)—especially for the People of the Book—have also been explained in their respective places. The introduction of the preceding Surah (An-Naml) also began with more or less these same words. This is a clear contextual indication that the central theme of both Surahs is one and the same.

The actual purpose of relating the story of Moses and Pharaoh:
Although the address is to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the final words of the verse, 'for a people who believe' (li-qawmin yu'minoon), are themselves witness that the purpose of relating this story is to open the ears of the Pharaohs of the time—i.e., the leaders of the Quraysh. 'In truth' (bil-haqq) means completely accurate in terms of its target, objective, lesson, and advice. In the Torah, the story of Moses and Pharaoh is neither narrated accurately nor does it present the moral lesson that is its true spirit. The Qur'an has related this story keeping both of these aspects in view. In our opinion, the verb in 'for a people who believe' carries the meaning of the intent of the action. This phrase contains a type of warning that we are relating this story, but its benefit will reach only those who possess the intent to believe. Those who have chosen to remain blind and deaf will continue to remain so.

Before the actual story, this and the subsequent two verses are introduced to present the objective and purpose for which it is being narrated. This style is adopted at several places in the Qur'an: before relating any narrative, the objective behind it is placed before the reader in brief words so that the central reality does not slip from the reader's view amidst the details of the story. An example of this has passed in Surah al-Kahf.

'Indeed, Pharaoh exalted himself in the land' (Inna Fir'awna 'ala fil-ard). Exalting oneself (rebellion) in the land means that a person, ignoring the will and commands of the actual Creator and Owner of the earth, begins to enforce his own absolute will and starts enslaving God's servants to himself instead of leading them to the worship and obedience of God.

'And made its people into sects, weakening a group among them' (Wa ja'ala ahlaha shiya'an yastad'ifu ta'ifatam-minhum). This is the explanation of his self-exaltation and corruption on earth: he divided the inhabitants of the country into classes and completely suppressed and enslaved one group among them. Since the ruler and the ruled are all the property of God, it is not permissible for any ruler of a land to practice discrimination or create divisions among his subjects; rather, there should be one law and one system of justice and equality for both the ruler and the ruled without any distinction. But Pharaoh had relegated the Children of Israel to the status of slaves and claimed divinity for himself. Along with this, he had given his own people, the Copts, the authority to exploit the Children of Israel like slaves.

'Slaughtering their sons and keeping their females alive' (yudhabbihu abna'ahum wa yastahyi nisa'ahum) is an example of the tyranny and oppression being inflicted on the Children of Israel at the hands of Pharaoh and the Copts. The cold-blooded political plan to slaughter the boys of the Children of Israel, initiated by Pharaoh and his chiefs out of political anxiety, has been explained in Surah Ta-Ha and some other previous Surahs.

'Indeed, he was of the corrupters' (Innahu kana minal-mufsideen) means he was of those who spread corruption in God's earth, whereas Allah bestows the station of authority upon anyone in His earth solely for the establishment of justice and peace.

The divine decree of granting leadership to Israel:
In accordance with the standard rules of Arabic grammar, an auxiliary verb is omitted before 'We willed' (Nureedu). The meaning is that while Pharaoh and his chiefs were inflicting these tyrannies and doing their utmost to prevent the Children of Israel from ever rising, Our will was to bestow favor upon the oppressed, grant them the station of leadership, and destroy the oppressors to grant the oppressed inheritance and succession. 'And make them leaders' (naj'alahum a'immah) refers to the religious leadership that the Children of Israel attained after the commission of Moses, and 'and make them heirs' (naj'alahumul-waritheen) refers to the political authority and kingdom they attained in the land of Palestine, which expanded extensively during the reign of Prophet Solomon (peace be upon him), to the extent that even the government of Egypt became a tributary state of their empire.

The divine decree of establishing the Children of Israel in power:
'Establishment in the land' (Tamkeen fil-ard) refers to political authority, strength, and majesty. That is, the divine will was to grant these suppressed and oppressed people a strong and powerful kingdom, and to show Pharaoh, Haman, and their hosts exactly what they feared. 'That which they feared' (ma kanoo yahdharoon) refers to the political anxiety of Pharaoh and his chiefs, which we have explained in its respective place: they were highly terrified of the constant increase in the population of the Children of Israel, fearing that if they gained strength, they would either capture the country themselves or ally with external enemies to expel the Copts. To counter this threat, these foolish people devised the plan of slaughtering the male children of the Israelites, as detailed in previous Surahs. However, against the plan of Allah, all their plans and preemptive measures proved completely useless. The divine will was fulfilled in favor of the oppressed, and their enemies were crushed despite all their power, majesty, and administrative skills. It should be noted that this reality is presented before the chiefs and leaders of the Quraysh through the upcoming narrative, so that they do not listen to this story merely as a tale, but keep in mind the truth inherent in it.

Haman was Pharaoh's minister:
Here, for the very first time, Haman is mentioned alongside Pharaoh, and in a manner that indicates his status was that of Pharaoh's minister, who held a specific interest in keeping the Children of Israel suppressed. Ahead too, he is mentioned in the capacity of Pharaoh's prime minister. This name does not appear in the Torah, but that makes no difference. There are many matters in which the Qur'an has corrected the narratives of the Torah or made additions to them. This too is a valuable addition to the story of Moses and Pharaoh. Some orientalists have made this name a target of objection, claiming that there was no person by this name in Egypt. This objection is completely absurd. Can these gentlemen claim that they have obtained a complete directory of the names of Pharaoh, all his ministers, chiefs, and the prominent figures of Egypt of that era? Leave aside the ministers and chiefs, do these gentlemen even agree on the identity of the specific Pharaoh who was the contemporary of Moses? Such statements made in the frenzy of opposing the Qur'an are completely unworthy of attention. These people are highly skilled in obscuring established facts, but when they present their own findings, it is clear that they have merely tried to place an elephant's shell over a grasshopper's leg.

Pharaoh's military preparations against the Israelite threat:
Here, the repeated mention of their armies alongside Pharaoh and Haman has a specific reason. The study of the history of that era shows that Pharaoh and his chiefs had given the issue of the Children of Israel a completely militarized political color. They regarded their large numbers as a threat to their government, which is why Pharaoh had commanded all his chiefs and nobles to keep their forces fully alert in their respective regions to counter the Israelite threat. Consequently, when he decided to pursue the Children of Israel, he summoned all his nobles and governors along with their forces. Pharaoh was highly proud of these armies, as is clear from both the Qur'an and the Torah, but Allah destroyed all this pride in the blink of an eye.

The beginning of the actual narrative:
Now the actual narrative begins: when Moses (peace be upon him) was born, We directed his mother through inspiration: 'Suckle him, and when you fear for him, cast him into the river and do not fear or grieve; indeed, We will restore him to you and will make him one of the messengers.'

Inspiration (wahy) here obviously does not refer to the technical revelation specific to the noble prophets, but to an instinction, dream, or casting of a thought into the heart in a manner that grants the heart peace and reassurance.

Divine arrangements for the protection of Moses:
During the era in which Moses was born, the plan to destroy the male children of the Israelites was being implemented with full force. Initially, Pharaoh and his chiefs tried to accomplish this through the midwives, but the Torah records that the midwives did not cooperate. Consequently, Pharaoh issued a general decree to the Copts to throw every male child born to the Israelites into the river. Moses was born during this highly perilous period. Therefore, his mother's heart was constantly in state of anxiety, fearing that some tyrant might spot the child and seize him. Pointing to this danger, the instruction was given: 'For now, suckle him, and when you sense any such danger, cast the child into the river with your own hands, without any fear or grief. We will surely return him to you and will bless him with messengership in the future.' The instruction to cast him into the river was obviously given because Allah chose that the very path Pharaoh had designated for the destruction of the children should become the path of salvation for Moses. Surah Ta-Ha (verse 39) also indicates that she was instructed to place the child in a chest and cast it into the river. The Torah describes this event as follows:

'The woman became pregnant and gave birth to a son. When she saw that he was a fine child, she hid him for three months. But when she could hide him no longer, she got a papyrus basket for him and coated it with tar and pitch. Then she placed the child in it and put it among the reeds along the bank of the Nile.' (Exodus 2:2-3)

We have pointed to the omissions and errors in the Torah's narrative in the commentary of Surah Ta-Ha and Surah al-A'raf. The key point of focus here is that Moses' mother was reassured through inspiration that 'We will return this child to you and will elevate him to the station of messengership,' and this reassurance was given in the form of a definitive promise. It was this trust in the promise of her Lord that enabled Moses' mother to take this daring step; otherwise, how could any mother cast her own child into the waves of a river?

Moses in the royal palace:
Based on contextual clues, some details are omitted here: that Moses' mother, upon sensing danger, placed the child in a papyrus chest and cast it into the River Nile, in accordance with the divine direction. If we rely on the Torah's narrative, the details show that she managed to hide the child for three months, but finally realized that this could not continue. Consequently, she had to take the step mentioned above. The River Nile flowed past the Israelite settlement toward the palace of Pharaoh, where the waves carried the chest to the shore. The people of Pharaoh's household spotted it, and upon finding a beautiful infant inside, brought him to the palace on the order of the King and Queen. Pharaoh's wife, as is clear from Surah at-Tahreem, was highly righteous. She pleaded: 'Do not kill him; perhaps he may benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son.' Thus, Moses reached the royal palace and became the comfort of the eyes of both the King and Queen. The word 'Al' (household/people) suggests that when the news of an infant in a chest reached the palace, all members of the royal household, small and great, gathered at the spot and brought him inside.

The secrets of divine decree are unknown to humans:
'That he might become to them an enemy and a grief' (Li-yakoona lahum 'aduwwan-wa hazana). We have explained this 'li' (so that) in another place: it is used to denote the ultimate consequence. They picked up the child so that he might become, as the Queen said, a comfort to their eyes, but they did not know the secret of the divine decree: that this very child would bring about the ultimate destruction of Pharaoh's empire.

'Indeed, Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were mistaken' (Inna Fir'awna wa Hamana wa junoodahuma kanoo khati'een) is a general comment on the conduct of Pharaoh, Haman, and their armies. In their foolishness, they thought that all power and authority was in their hands and that they would keep the Children of Israel suppressed forever. They had no idea that if Allah willed, He would have the one who would crush their power raised in their own royal palace, by the hands of the King and Queen themselves! Review the commentary on verse 6.

Pharaoh's wife was highly righteous:
When the Queen saw the child, she was drawn to his beautiful face. She said to Pharaoh: 'He is a comfort of the eye for me and for you. Do not kill him; perhaps he may benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son.' We have explained elsewhere that the Queen was highly righteous and deeply averse to Pharaoh's conduct. 'Perhaps he may benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son' are the exact same words used by the Al-Aziz of Egypt to his wife regarding Prophet Joseph. This suggests that the Queen had no children of her own at that time, and if we accept the Torah's account that the one who first spotted the chest was Pharaoh's daughter, it must be assumed that they had no male heir. Therefore, they hoped that such a beautiful child would bring them blessings, or they might adopt him as a son. 'And they perceived not' (Wa hum la yash'uroon) means while the King and Queen were thinking of their own plans, they had no idea that power was preparing to display its grand glory through this very child!

Allah's treatment of the believers in trials:
When Moses' mother saw no other way, she gathered her courage and cast the chest into the river, but this passage points to what she experienced afterward: her heart became completely void of patience and composure. She was on the verge of disclosing the secret out of anxiety, but Allah strengthened her heart so that she might remain firm in her faith during this trial. A human being is, after all, human; for any mother to cast her own child into the waves of a river with her own hands is not an easy thing. Though she had the support of a divine inspiration—without which she could never have conceived such an action—when we ourselves feel our hearts tremble merely by imagining this event, who else but her Lord could fathom what Moses' mother experienced at that moment! But Allah strengthened her at that critical juncture, as only He can. Allah subjects His servants to trials, which is His established law, and these trials can be extremely severe depending on the status of the person. However, it is also His law that He supports those who show courage in His trials. Since Moses' mother had proven her faith and trust by casting the child into the river, the Merciful Lord strengthened her heart in the next phase to safeguard her faith and trust, preventing any word from escaping that might compromise it. This is how Allah deals with His believing servants, though few understand this!

The final measure for peace of mind:
When the chest floated away on the water, she said to Moses' sister: 'Follow him.' This was the final measure she could take for her peace of mind. Obviously, following the chest was possible only up to a certain limit, and it had to disappear from sight eventually, but how could she deprive herself of watching it for as long and as far as possible! Consequently, Moses' sister kept watch from a distance in a manner that did not draw anyone's attention to the chest. It was Allah's grace that her mission succeeded: she witnessed the chest reaching the shore near the palace and her baby brother being taken inside, which indicates that the royal palace was not very far from the Israelite settlement.

Another divine arrangement:
This describes the plan Allah utilized to return Moses to his mother. It was mentioned above that the child was taken into the palace with great affection. Naturally, the first concern was to find a wet nurse. When a wet nurse was brought, Moses refused her milk. Another was brought, then a third, but Moses refused to nurse from any of them. Moses' sister took advantage of this situation. She stepped forward and said: 'Shall I direct you to a household that will rear him for you and look after him with complete care and affection?' Since the palace people were anxious, they immediately agreed, opening the way for Moses to return to the lap of his mother.

Moses back in his mother's lap - the core lesson:
Through this arrangement, the Creator and Merciful Lord rescued Moses from the river and returned him to the lap of his mother, so that her eyes might be comforted and she might not grieve.

'And that she might know that the promise of Allah is true, but most of them do not know' (Wa li-ta'lama anna wa'dallahi haqqun wa lakinna aktharahum la ya'lamoon). This points to the fulfillment of the promise mentioned in verse 7: 'Cast him into the river, and We will protect him and return him to you.' The text states: 'Thus We showed her that We fulfill our promises, regardless of how improbable their fulfillment may appear, and Our plan never fails.' 'But most of them do not know' points to the core lesson: the majority of people, due to their short-sightedness, do not understand this reality. They regard God's promises as mere wishful thinking and see risk in taking any step based on trust in Him. They want to see the fulfillment of Allah's promises before they believe, whereas the actual trial in this world is to live and die for those promises of the Lord whose physical reality is yet to manifest.

The true beauty of Moses' youth:
'When he reached maturity' (Bulugha ashuddahu) refers to reaching youth, and 'and became established' (wastawa) points to intellectual and emotional balance and moderation. That is, when Moses (peace be upon him) reached physical youth and attained intellectual and emotional maturity. It should be noted that youth in itself is of no value unless accompanied by the beauty of intellectual and emotional balance.

'Hukm' refers to the power of judgment, and 'ilm' refers to the recognition of God. The highest degree of this judgment and knowledge is that granted to the noble prophets. In the Qur'an, these exact words are used for the knowledge of the prophets, but here it obviously refers to the wisdom and knowledge granted to Moses in his early life, as his prophethood was bestowed much later, as detailed ahead.

The text states that when Moses reached youth and attained maturity and balance of intellect and temperament, We blessed him with judgment and knowledge. 'And thus We reward the doers of good' (Wa kadhalika najzee mabsaneen). Those who act uprightly are rewarded in this manner. 'Doers of good' (muhsineen) refers to those who utilize their faculties and capabilities in the correct manner. The text states that such people are blessed with His knowledge and wisdom, which is the true beauty and perfection of human faculties. If a person fails to attain this wisdom, he has wasted both his capabilities and his youth.

Verses 15-16
Meaning of 'the city':
Since the Israelites were slaves and laborers, their settlement was built separate from the main city. They would enter the city only during working hours as laborers.

Upright youth in the field of action:
When Moses (peace be upon him) reached youth and developed the strength, chivalry, compassion, and sense of justice that characterize upright youth, he began to visit the city occasionally to check on his oppressed brethren. However, since his sense of justice made him a target of suspicion for the Copts, he had to do this cautiously, entering the city at times when people were resting. One day, while walking through the city, he saw a Copt and an Israelite fighting. Upon seeing Moses, the Israelite appealed to him for help. Out of chivalry and a desire to support the oppressed, Moses stepped forward to intervene, but the Copt resisted. In self-defense, Moses struck him with a blow that proved fatal, and the Copt died on the spot. Moses had no intention of killing the Copt, nor did he expect such an outcome. This unintended tragedy filled him with deep regret, and he immediately prayed to his Lord: 'My Lord, indeed I have wronged myself, so forgive me.' Since this mistake was entirely unintentional and he sought forgiveness immediately, Allah forgave him and blessed him with reassurance. He is indeed All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

It was mentioned above that Allah had blessed Moses with youth, judgment, and knowledge; this incident displays that wisdom. If Moses were merely a strong youth, he would have felt proud of his physical strength, especially after killing a Copt. Instead, he treated this unintentional mistake as a sin and sought immediate forgiveness, which is the conduct of the righteous. This was a matter entirely between Moses and his Lord; under the prevailing system of Egypt, Moses could expect no justice from the authorities.

Vigilance for the future:
Blessings inspire the righteous to greater gratitude. When Allah blessed Moses with forgiveness, he made a vow: 'My Lord, for the favor You have bestowed upon me, I will never be an assistant to the criminals.' This does not mean he had assisted the Israelite knowing him to be a criminal; he had acted to help him as an oppressed person, assuming the Copt was the oppressor. However, when Allah forgave him but still treated the act as a mistake requiring forgiveness, Moses concluded that he had acted without full investigation and had inadvertently supported a wrongdoer. Therefore, he vowed never to support anyone without full investigation, and to assist only those whose status as oppressed was established. Consequently, when he saw the same Israelite fighting another Copt the next day and seeking his help, Moses rebuked him: 'Indeed, you are a troublemaker.'

The next day, Moses entered the city in a state of fear and vigilance. While he had always been cautious, he was now even more anxious about the consequences of the incident and what was being discussed among the people. Suddenly, he saw the same Israelite who had sought his help the previous day fighting again and crying out for assistance. Moses rebuked him: 'Indeed, you are an open troublemaker.' A clear proof of his being a troublemaker was that he had been fighting yesterday, leading to a tragedy, and was fighting again today. This was a clear indication of his rebellious nature. Furthermore, based on the lesson he had drawn from Allah's forgiveness, Moses recognized his mistake in assisting him yesterday. In line with his vow never to support a criminal, he did not rush to help him today; instead, he rebuked him, saying: 'You are indeed a troublemaker.'

A stylistic feature:
Based on Arabic usage, a verb is omitted before 'When' (Falamma), meaning: 'When the situation reached this point...' This indicates that Moses first tried to settle the issue verbally, but when the Copt resisted, Moses stepped forward to restrain him. The Copt is described as 'an enemy to both of them' because he was an active enemy to the Israelite, and was also an enemy to Moses due to the national division and the suspicion Moses had drawn upon himself.

Exposure of the secret due to the Israelite's foolishness:
Moses stepped forward to restrain the Copt, but since he had just rebuked the Israelite, the Israelite panicked, thinking Moses was going to strike him. In his fear, he cried out: 'O Moses, do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday?' Thus, through his foolishness, he exposed the secret. Some commentators have attributed this statement to the Copt, but this is unlikely because the killing was still a secret, and the Copt would not have referred to Moses as a reformer.

'You only intend to be a tyrant in the land, and you do not intend to be of the reformers' (In tureedu illa an takoona jabbaran fil-ard wa ma tureedu an takoona minal-musliheen) is a personal attack on Moses. This shows the character of low-minded people: as long as someone helps them, they consider him a reformer, but the moment he restrains them from evil, they label him a tyrant and are ready to expose his secrets and act as informants. It also shows Moses' character: even at that early stage, he was regarded as a reformer and a source of hope among his people.

The conduct of a believer:
Moses was already a target of suspicion for the Copts, but when this incident occurred and was exposed by the Israelite, the anger of the Copts was fully aroused, and they began planning his execution.

'And a man came from the farthest end of the city, running' (Wa ja'a rajulum-min aqsal-madeenati yas'a). Since the main city where the government offices were located was separate from the Israelite settlement, it is described as 'the farthest end of the city.' The text states that a man, having learned that the authorities were planning Moses' execution, ran to Moses and warned him: 'The chiefs are counseling to kill you, so escape; indeed, I am to you of the sincere advisors.' While no details are mentioned here, Surah Ghafir details the character of a believer from the family of Pharaoh who supported Moses in the court. Since he was from the royal family and supported Moses, it is highly likely that this reference is to him.

His words 'Indeed, I am to you of the sincere advisors' were to assure Moses of his sincerity, as the prevailing suspicion between the Israelites and the Copts might have led Moses to think a Copt was trying to intimidate him. The word 'running' (yas'a) highlights that the moment he learned of the danger, he ran to the Israelite settlement to warn Moses, displaying his deep concern and sincerity.

نخرج منھا خآئفا یترقب قال رب نجنی من القوم الظلمین 21
Following this, Moses (peace be upon him) escaped from Egypt in a state of fear and vigilance, praying: 'My Lord, deliver me from the wrongdoing people.'

Moses sets out for Madyan - a lesson for those who work in the way of Allah:
The style shows that when Moses escaped from Egypt, he had not determined his destination. He subsequently decided to set out for Madyan, but even then, he had no specific destination or person in mind there. He simply set out with trust in Allah, praying: 'Perhaps my Lord will guide me to the sound path.' It should be noted that this escape was not the migration (hijrah) that occurred after his formal commission. In the case of migration, the divine law is that the time and destinations are determined by Allah through revelation, but in this journey, Moses had no such explicit guidance. He simply set out with trust in Allah, and his trust became his guide. This is highly instructive for those who face trials in the path of truth: if they take steps with trust in Allah, He will guide them even if they receive no explicit inspiration beforehand.

Meaning of 'water':
'Water' (ma') refers to a spring or a well. The Torah mentions a well. Mountain springs and wells are similar, and accessing them can be challenging, especially when there is a large crowd.

An event that touched Moses' heart:
When Moses (peace be upon him) reached Madyan, he sat down by the well. There he saw a crowd of shepherds watering their flocks, while two women were keeping their sheep back from the water. The word 'tadhodhan' suggests that while their sheep were eager to reach the water, the women had to struggle to keep them back. Moses asked them: 'What is your concern? Why are you keeping your sheep back?' They replied: 'Our father is an old man, so we have to tend the flock ourselves, and we cannot enter the crowd of men to water our sheep. We must wait until the shepherds finish and leave; only then can we water our flock.'

Moses' chivalrous act:
Hearing their plight, Moses' sense of chivalry was aroused. He stepped forward, watered their sheep for them, and then returned to the shade where he had been sitting, praying: 'My Lord, indeed I am, for whatever good You would send down to me, in need.'

Key points of focus:
Several points are particularly noteworthy here:

First: Moses was struck by the fact that two women had to perform the hard task of tending sheep and were standing helplessly by the well. His concern is reflected in his question: 'What is your concern?' (ma khatbukuma). 'Khatb' in Arabic is used for a grave and important issue, which shows that his question carried: 'What is the plight that has forced you to perform this hard task and stand helplessly here?' The women understood his question and replied: 'Our father is an old man, and we cannot enter this crowd of men.' This shows that even in that era, the spheres of work for men and women were separate, and if women had to perform out-of-doors tasks due to necessity, they did so out of compulsion and with great caution, keeping separate from men rather than mixing with them. Contrast this with the claims of modern social reformers who argue that the spheres of men and women are identical and that they should work alongside each other in every field, citing the example of rural women but applying the conclusion to urban ladies.

Second: Moses performed his duty of helping the weak with full energy, as was his character, but did not utter a single word to the women to express his own plight or need. Instead, he returned to the shade where he had been sitting, praying: 'My Lord, I have reached the place I set out for; now I wait for Your grace and mercy. I am in need of whatever good You send down to me.' The eloquence of this prayer is beyond description. Since it was uttered at the right moment and with complete sincerity, it was immediately accepted: the women mentioned his help to their father, opening the way for the good he had prayed for.

The opening of the way for Moses:
Moses was still sitting in the shade when one of the two women returned, walking with shyness. She said: 'Indeed, my father invites you that he may reward you for watering our sheep.' The Torah records that because they returned home earlier than usual, their father, Prophet Shu'ayb, asked: 'How is it that you have returned so early today?' They replied: 'An Egyptian helped us and watered our sheep for us.' Shu'ayb said: 'Where is he? Go and invite him to eat bread with us.' In compliance with her father's command, one of the daughters went to Moses and invited him. Since Moses was in need of help, he recognized this as divine support and went with her.

The conduct of noble women:
The words 'walking with shyness' (tamshee 'alastihya') are highly significant. It was mentioned earlier that they were standing separate from the shepherds, and that they did so to avoid mixing with men. Now, the text states that when she came to invite Moses, she did so with shyness, wrapping herself in her clothes and maintaining proper distance, rather than approaching him in an bold manner. Why does the Qur'an describe these details with such precision? It is to show the standard of noble conduct and how women should behave around men. These are the noble manners that the Qur'an has formulated into laws in Surah an-Noor and Surah al-Ahzab, which we have explained in Surah an-Noor. It should be noted that this was the conduct of women who had to tend sheep; what then should be the conduct of women who have no such compulsion!

فلما جآءہ وقص علیہ القصص قال لاتخف نجوت من القوم الظلمین 
Moses before Prophet Shu'ayb:
Moses arrived before Shu'ayb and related his entire story. Shu'ayb reassured him: 'Do not fear. You have escaped from the wrongdoing people.' Thus, Moses received the glad tidings of the acceptance of the prayer he had made upon escaping from Egypt: 'My Lord, deliver me from the wrongdoing people.'

The repetition of the subject 'one of them' (ihdahuma) suggests that this recommendation was made by the other daughter, not the one who had gone to invite Moses. Had it been the same daughter, there would be no need to repeat the subject.

Character is recognized at first glance:
Shu'ayb was indeed in need of a helper to tend his flock and was looking for a suitable person. Therefore, the daughter recommended: 'O my father, hire him. Indeed, the best of everyone you hire is the strong and trustworthy.' While physical strength is obvious and can be recognized at first glance, trustworthiness is a matter of character that usually requires long experience. However, in certain situations, character is recognized at first glance; a person's eyes and expressions testify to his character. The daughter had already witnessed Moses' chivalry, selflessness, and respectful conduct, and when she heard his story, she recognized that a man of such determination and honor must be highly trustworthy.

Shu'ayb's proposal:
Shu'ayb made a proposal to Moses: 'Indeed, I wish to wed you one of these, my two daughters, on the condition that you serve me for eight years; and if you complete ten, it will be of your own accord. I do not wish to make it difficult for you. Indeed, you will find me, if Allah wills, of the righteous.' This proposal was likely based on a divine inspiration, and his words 'I do not wish to make it difficult for you' allowed Moses time to consider the proposal freely, without feeling pressured to make a decision.

There is no need for a lengthy discussion on the issue of dowry here. Dowry is the right of the woman, and if her guardian proposes a valid condition with her consent, there is no legal objection to it.

Moses accepts the proposal:
Moses (peace be upon him) accepted the proposal and the condition, saying: 'That is between me and you. Whichever of the two terms I complete, there is no injustice to me, and Allah is witness over what we say.' Here, 'say' refers to their covenant and agreement.

Return to Egypt and the vision at Mount Toor:
After completing the agreed term, Moses (peace be upon him) set out for Egypt with his family. The Qur'an and the Torah do not specify whether he completed the eight-year or ten-year term, but the Torah records that he was accompanied by his wife and two sons. When they reached the vicinity of Mount Toor, the night was dark and cold, and they lost their way. Suddenly, Moses spotted a light from the direction of Mount Toor. He said to his family: 'Stay here; indeed, I perceive a fire. Perhaps I will bring you from it some information or a burning brand of fire that you may warm yourselves.'

The explanation of all these details has passed in Surah Ta-Ha and Surah An-Naml. When Moses reached the spot, a voice called out to him from the right side of the valley, in the blessed place, from the tree: 'O Moses, indeed I am Allah, Lord of the worlds.'

Three locations are mentioned to define the origin of the voice: from the right side of the valley, in the blessed place, and from the tree. This is to show that Moses did not hear a vague voice echoing in space, but a distinct voice coming from a specific direction, a blessed valley, and a blessed tree. A place or object being described as blessed means it was chosen by Allah for His manifestation, and was thus protected from any satanic interference. Review the commentary on Surah An-Naml, verse 8.

The first voice Moses heard:
'Indeed, I am Allah, Lord of the worlds' (Innee Anallah Rabbul-Alameen). The previous words described the sanctity of the place, and these are the first words Moses heard. He was told: 'You came to fetch a brand of fire, but there is no fire here; indeed, I am Allah, Lord of the worlds.' This was Allah's introduction of His Essence. The concept of 'Lord of the worlds' is comprehensive, and is expressed in other places as: 'Indeed, I am Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'

The first miracle:
Along with this, he was commanded: 'Throw down your staff.' When Moses complied, he saw the staff moving like a snake. This sight terrified him, and he fled without looking back. 'Without looking back' (wa lam yu'aqqib) has been explained under Surah An-Naml.

The effect of initial spiritual experiences on prophets:
The initial spiritual experiences of the prophets are completely unexpected, without any prior aspiration, which is why they initially feel anxious. Allah gradually reassures them. In contrast, fortune-tellers, sorcerers, and impostors have a pre-planned scheme, undergoing great efforts to learn their tricks and treating any success as a triumph. But the prophets are free from such deceit. The magicians of Egypt, to whom Moses was sent, underwent great efforts to make ropes appear like snakes and felt proud of it, but when Allah turned Moses' staff into a snake, he fled in fear. Allah has highlighted this aspect of Moses' initial experience to show the difference between his true miracle and the sorcery of the Egyptians.

'O Moses, draw near and do not fear. Indeed, you are of the secure' (Ya Musa aqbil wa la takhaf inna minal-amineen). When Moses panicked, Allah reassured him with mercy: 'Step forward and pick it up; it holds no danger for you. The danger is for your enemies; you are completely secure.' Surah An-Naml expresses this as: 'Indeed, the messengers do not fear in My presence.' Meaning, you are My messenger, and We arm Our messengers; the danger is for Our enemies, not for Our messengers.

اسلک یدک فی جیباک تخرج بیضآء من غیر سوٓء واضمم الیک جناحک من الرھب فذنک برھانن من ربک الی فرعون وملا بہ الھمم کانوا قوماً فسقین (32)
The second miracle and its method:
This describes the method of the second miracle: 'Insert your hand into your bosom; it will come out white, without disease.' 'And draw in your arm close to you against fear' (Wadmum ilayka janahaka minar-rahb) describes the action: 'Just as a person draws in his arms close to him when afraid, draw in your hand close to your bosom.' Surah Ta-Ha describes this as:

'And draw your hand close to your side; it will come out white, without disease' (Ta-Ha: 22).

'Minar-rahb' (against fear) is used here in the same manner as 'minadh-dhull' (out of humility) in other places.

'For those are two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his establishment' (Fa-dhanika burhanani min Rabbika ila Fir'awna wa mala'ihi). A verb is omitted before 'to', meaning: 'Go, armed with these two clear proofs from your Lord, to Pharaoh and his establishment.' 'Indeed, they have been a defiantly disobedient people' explains why he was armed with these grand signs and sent to Pharaoh: because they had transgressed all limits of rebellion. 'Go, warn them of their rebellion and establish proof against them through these signs.'

An apprehension of Moses:
Moses (peace be upon him) accepted the command, but expressed his apprehension: 'My Lord, indeed I killed a person among them, so I fear they will kill me.' He meant: 'Though I am returning to Egypt after eight or ten years, they likely remember the incident, and when I stand before them as a messenger, they will not forgive me.'

'Rid'' means helper or assistant, expressed as 'wazir' (minister) in other places.

'That he may support me' (yusaddiqunee) means to confirm my truthfulness.

Request for Aaron's assistance:
Moses expressed another difficulty. We noted in Surah Ta-Ha that he felt a lack of eloquence in his speech, which is why he requested: 'My brother Aaron is more eloquent than me, so send him with me as a helper to confirm my truthfulness.' In Surah Ta-Ha, this is expressed as: 'That we may glorify You much and remember You much' (Ta-Ha: 33-34). 'Indeed, I fear they will deny me' means they will try to reject my words, so bless me with his assistance so that we may establish proof against them together.

'Sultan' here means dominance, authority, and majesty.

There are two possibilities regarding 'through Our signs': either it is connected to 'We will grant you dominance,' meaning: 'We will establish your dominance over the Egyptians through Our miracles,' or it is preceded by an omitted verb, as in verse 32.

Moses' awe over the Egyptians: Allah reassured him: 'Rest assured; We will establish such awe of you in their hearts that they will not dare to cause you any harm.' Indeed, Pharaoh and his chiefs were so awed by Moses that they never dared to execute him, despite their intense hatred. The reason, as clear from the Torah, was that they were internally convinced of Moses' truthfulness, but refused to accept his call because it opposed their interests. They knew that harming him would invite His wrath, so they refrained from killing him. The Torah records that whenever a plague struck, they would beg Moses to pray to his Lord to remove it.

When Moses arrived before Pharaoh and his chiefs with these clear proofs, they dismissed his miracles as mere magic and started propaganda against his call of monotheism, saying: 'We have never heard of this among our ancient forefathers.' 'Fabricated magic' (sihrum-muftara) means they asserted that Moses performed these tricks through magic, but falsely claimed they were miracles from God. 'We have never heard of this among our ancient forefathers' refers to his call to the 'Lord of the worlds': 'His claim of being a messenger of the Lord of the worlds is completely novel; we have never heard of such a thing from our ancestors.' This is explained in verse 38, and is identical to: 'We have never heard of this in the latest religion.'

Moses' challenge regarding the future:
This is Moses' challenge regarding the future, expressed in a highly polite and dignified manner: 'My Lord knows best who has come with guidance from Him, and who are knowingly rejecting it. You call me an impostor, but the future will show who attains the ultimate home and who is defeated.' It means: 'I and my companions will surely prevail, while you will be humiliated.'

'Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed' (Innahu la yuflihuz-zalimoon) means those who reject God's guidance are unjust to themselves, and the unjust will never find success. The matching sentence is omitted in Arabic, but has been clarified in the translation.

Haman has been mentioned previously; here too, it appears he was Pharaoh's prime minister or minister of construction.

'Kindle for me, O Haman, upon the clay' (fa-awqid lee ya-Hamanu 'alat-teen) could refer to baking bricks or constructing a tall tower and heating it. History records that in ancient Nineveh and Egypt, a method of construction involved building with clay and heating it to make it durable and weather-resistant.

In 'that I may look at the God of Moses' (la'allee attali'u ila ilahi Musa), the preposition 'ila' indicates an omission, meaning: 'that I may climb the tower and look at the God of Moses.' Pharaoh said this purely to mock Moses' words. Surah az-Zukhruf, verse 47, also shows that this was a mockery.

Pharaoh first said to his courtiers: 'I know of no god for you other than myself. I do not know of this "Lord of the worlds" whom Moses claims to represent. Therefore, remain alert against his trial.' Then, turning to Haman, he said: 'Kindle the clay to build a high tower, so that I may climb it and look at this God of Moses! I believe he is a liar.' It is common for tyrants to mock obvious truths, and their blind followers treat this mockery as an argument. It is not surprising if the Egyptians believed him, as they regarded him as the incarnation of the sun god, who could easily look into the heavens. Surah Ghafir, verses 36-37, records his words: 'O Haman, build for me a tower that I may reach the ways—the ways of the heavens.' This was also a plan to deceive his own people.

واستکبر ھروجنودہ فی الارض بغیر الحق وظنوا انھما لینا لایرجعون 39
Arrogance without right:
The meaning of 'without right' (bighayril-haqq) has been explained. The only One who has the right to show pride in this universe is the One who created it and runs its system. Those who have no share in its creation or administration have no right to show arrogance. Such arrogance is quickly punished by the Creator. It should be noted that enforcing any law contrary to the law of the supreme Ruler of the universe is also a form of 'arrogance without right,' which is identical to the way of Pharaoh.

'And they thought that they would not be returned to Us' (wa zannoo annahum ilayna la yurja'oon) defines the cause of their arrogance: they thought they were free to do as they pleased and would never be called to account before Him.

The end of arrogance:
This describes the end of their arrogance: 'So We seized him and his hosts and threw them into the sea.' The plan Allah utilized to seize them has been detailed in previous Surahs, particularly Surah Ta-Ha. 'So see what was the end of the wrongdoers' (fanzur kayfa kana 'aqibatuz-zalimeen) draws attention to the primary lesson of the story: it offers consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and a warning to the leaders of the Quraysh who were adopting the exact same attitude.

Here, 'We made' (ja'alnahum) carries the meaning of 'We granted them respite,' and an auxiliary verb is omitted before 'calling' (yad'oon).

This further details the end of their arrogance: We granted them respite in this world, and they remained leaders calling to the Fire, and on the Day of Judgment, they will not be helped. The followers they led in this world will abandon them, and everyone will be concerned only with themselves; neither the leaders can help the followers, nor the followers the leaders.

In the world where they held leadership and had people shout slogans for them, We pursued them with a curse, and on the Day of Judgment, they will be among the despised. This same theme appeared in Surah Hud:

'And they followed the command of Pharaoh, and the command of Pharaoh was not right. He will precede his people on the Day of Resurrection and lead them into the Fire, and wretched is the place to which they are led. And they were pursued in this [world] with a curse and on the Day of Resurrection. Wretched is the gift which is given.' (Hud: 97-99)

Favor upon the oppressed:
After describing the end of the wrongdoers, this mentions the favor bestowed upon the oppressed, which was alluded to in the introduction: 'And We willed to bestow favor upon those who were oppressed...' (verse 5). The text states: 'And We gave Moses the Scripture after We had destroyed the former generations.' The attributes of the Scripture are described: it was a source of enlightenment, guidance, and mercy. 'Enlightenment' (basa'ir) is used in the plural to highlight that it contained clear signs and arguments that opened the eyes. The combination 'guidance and mercy' means guidance in this life and mercy in the hereafter.

It should be noted that a nation being granted a Scripture is akin to being granted leadership of the world, provided they value it. The Children of Israel were the first to receive this blessing. 'After We had destroyed the former generations' (min ba'di ma ahlaknal-quroonal-oola) shows that a formal Book of law was first given to the Children of Israel through Moses, but they treated it with great neglect, as detailed in Surah al-Baqarah.

Proof of the Prophet's messengership:
At the end of the narrative, this and the subsequent verses are addressed to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to prove his prophethood, targeting both the Jews and the Quraysh. It states: 'And you were not on the western side of the Mount when We decreed the matter to Moses, nor were you among the witnesses who waited for Moses' return.'

'On the western side' refers to the western side of Mount Toor.

'We decreed to Moses the matter' (qadayna ila Musal-amr). 'Qadayna' here carries the meaning of 'We made a covenant.'

'Witnesses' (shahideen) refers to Moses' people who waited at the foot of the mount while Moses went to receive the Torah. During his absence, the trial of the golden calf occurred, as detailed in previous Surahs.

The message is: since you were not present when Moses was granted the Torah, how did you obtain this detailed and accurate account of these events? It is a clear proof that Allah has revealed these matters to you, and that you are His messenger.

It should be noted that the story of Moses is narrated in the Qur'an with such detail and accuracy as is not found in the Torah. If you compare this portion of the narrative with the Torah's account, you will find that the Torah's version is disjointed and lacks the spiritual essence of the story. How did the Prophet learn these accurate details? Suggesting that he copied them from the People of the Book is absurd, as any copied account would carry the same errors. The Qur'an's version is so consistent and logical that its wisdom and truth are obvious.

The opening part of this verse is connected to the previous one: 'You were not present at these events, but We informed you because after Moses, We produced many generations, and a long time passed over them, causing them to forget the Scripture We had given them.' Therefore, it was necessary to revive this guidance through you. Based on the context, words like 'and they forgot the reminder' are omitted after 'and a long time passed over them.'

'And you were not a dweller among the people of Madyan' means: 'Just as you were not present when Moses was granted the Torah, you were not present in Madyan to witness his years there, or the call of Shu'ayb and the end of his people. You were ignorant of all these matters, but We informed you so that We might make you a messenger, just as We sent messengers before.' 'We are the ones who send' (kunna mursileen) is identical in style to 'We are the ones who act' (kunna fa'ileen), which is used to denote a firm and definitive decree. Coupling 'dwellers of Madyan' with 'reciting to them Our verses' refers to the story of Shu'ayb detailed in other Surahs. The meaning is: 'You were not commissioned to warn those people, but We informed you of their history to make you a messenger. This is a clear proof of your messengership; unfortunate are those who do not understand this.'

Meaning: 'Just as you were not in Madyan, you were not by the side of the Mount when We called Moses' (referring to the call mentioned in verse 30). The text states: 'You were ignorant of all these matters, but His mercy chose to make you a messenger, revealing these matters to you as clear proofs of your messengership.' A verb is omitted before 'mercy' (rahmah).

The purpose of the Prophet's commission:
'That you may warn a people to whom no warner had come before you that perhaps they will be reminded' (Li-tundhira qawmam-ma atahum-min-nadeerim-min-qablika la'allahum yatadhakkaroon). This defines the purpose of his commission: to warn the Ishmaelites, to whom no messenger had been sent before, so that they might be reminded. They were an unlettered people, and no messenger had been commissioned among them. In accordance with Abraham's prayer, Allah raised this unlettered Prophet from among them. This commission is a great mercy for them if they value it, but it will turn into a source of punishment if they reject it, for the divine law is that a nation that rejects a warner is destroyed.

The upcoming subject... Verses 47-61
The previous verses made it clear that just as Allah sent Moses to Pharaoh, He has sent the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). His commission is a great mercy if they value it, and a source of punishment if they reject it. This upcoming discourse explains that the purpose of his commission is to establish proof, so that if they face punishment, they cannot claim they were not warned. However, they copy the objections of the Jews, asking why the Prophet has not been given physical miracles like those of Moses. Ask them: 'Did the people of Moses value his miracles, that we should expect you to believe if you were shown similar miracles?' Along with this, the sincere among the People of the Book are praised for accepting the Qur'an despite the opposition of their people. Furthermore, the Quraysh's fear that accepting the call would lead to their political ruin is addressed, warning them that accepting the call is their only security, while rejecting it will lead to their destruction. Read the verses in this light.

According to the rules of Arabic grammar, the response to 'If not' (Lawla) is omitted here, but has been clarified in the translation.

Favor and warning to the Ishmaelites:
This is a favor and a warning to the Quraysh: 'Had We willed, We could have punished them for their misdeeds without sending a messenger, but We did not do so, so that they might not excuse themselves, saying: "If only You had sent us a messenger, we would have followed Your verses and believed."' To eliminate this excuse and establish proof, We have sent Our Messenger. If they reject this favor, the proof has been established against them.

This shows that the intellect and nature Allah has granted to humans are sufficient to guide them away from deviation. Had Allah punished them based on natural guidance alone, it would not have been contrary to His justice, but to eliminate all excuses, He sent His messengers.

'The truth' (al-haqq) here refers to both the Messenger and the Book, as they are identical in essence. 'From Us' (min 'indina) highlights the grandeur of this truth: it originates from Allah, so everything that opposes it is false and destined to vanish.

An objection taught by the Jews and its response:
'But when the truth came to them from Us, they said: "Why was he not given the like of what was given to Moses?"'

Context indicates that while the Quraysh raised this objection, it was taught to them by the Jews, who were active behind the scenes. They told the Quraysh: 'This man claims to be a messenger, but messengers are accompanied by grand miracles. Our messenger was granted such miracles, so why has he not been given similar miracles?' The Quraysh, in their simplicity, copied this objection. The Qur'an responds: 'Did they not disbelieve in what was given to Moses before?' The verb is attributed to them due to their identical mindset, meaning: 'Since your hearts are like the hearts of the past deniers, your conduct is identical.' This response also targets the Jews who taught them this objection.

'They said: "Two works of magic supporting each other!"' (Qaloo sihrani tazahara). 'Sihr' here means magicians, and 'tazahara' means to cooperate and conspire. Meaning, they called Moses and Aaron magicians who had conspired against them, 'and they said: "Indeed, in both we are disbelievers"' (wa qaloo inna bikullin kafiroon), refusing to believe in them regardless of the signs they showed.

A challenge to the Quraysh:
Meaning: 'They refer to Moses' miracles to oppose you, but this is a mere excuse. If they are sincere, why did they not believe in Moses? Say: "Then bring a scripture from Allah which is more guiding than either of them, that I may follow it, if you should be truthful."' 

Seeking guidance is a natural demand:
Seeking guidance is a natural demand of sound human nature. On this basis, I believe in the Torah and the Qur'an, and if you produce a book more guiding than either, I will follow it. But your case is strange: you believe in neither, yet you raise this objection to avoid the truth.

Meaning: 'If they cannot produce a book more guiding than either and still refuse to follow them, it is clear that they only wish to follow their own desires. And who is more astray than one who follows his desire without guidance from Allah?' Desires seek only self-gratification and cannot distinguish between truth and falsehood, often overpowering human intellect. Therefore, the only path to safety is to regulate desires under the guidance of Allah; otherwise, one falls into destruction.

'Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people' (Innahu la yahdiyal-qawmaz-zalimeen) means He revealed His guidance to protect people from the destruction of desires, so unfortunate are those who ignore it and follow their desires. Such people are unjust to themselves, and are deprived of guidance.

The Qur'an is a familiar message to the People of the Book:
Meaning, Allah maintained the continuity of His guidance and never let it be interrupted. When the followers of Moses forgot the Torah, He blessed the unlettered Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) with this revelation. This is a restatement of the theme of verses 45-46. It means that the Qur'an is not a foreign or strange message, but a familiar one; therefore, the People of the Book should be the first to accept it, as it completes the guidance they received previously. The Ishmaelites too should value it, as they have been blessed with a direct revelation.

Verses 52-53
Commendation of the righteous among the People of the Book:
'Those to whom We gave the Scripture' refers to the righteous among them, as shown by the passive form of the verb and their conduct described here.

This points to a testimony in favor of the Qur'an: if some short-sighted people are turning away from it, do not worry about them. The righteous among the People of the Book are valuing this blessing and believing in it. When it is recited to them, they declare: 'We believe in it; indeed, it is the truth from our Lord. Indeed, we were, before it, Muslims.'

'Indeed, it is the truth from our Lord' (Innahul-haqqu mir-Rabbina) shows that they supported the Qur'an's corrections of their scriptures against the opposition of their own people, and advised the Quraysh to accept it. This is identical to: 'They recognize it as they recognize their own sons.'

'Indeed, we were, before it, Muslims' means they were already waiting for the arrival of this final Prophet and Book, in accordance with the predictions of their scriptures, and submitted to his authority the moment they recognized him.

The text states that these people will receive a double reward for their steadfastness: first, because they remained firm on the true teachings of Moses despite the deviation of their nation, and second, because they believed in the final Prophet predicted by their scriptures. 'Because they were patient' (bi-ma sabaroo) is highly significant: they had to face intense trials to remain firm on their previous faith, and when they embraced Islam, they had to face the opposition of both their own people and the Quraysh, yet they remained steadfast. The history of the early Christians who remained firm on the truth is well-known, and the Qur'an praises their sincerity in various places.

The character of the righteous among the People of the Book:
'And they ward off evil with good' (Wa yadra'oona bil-hasanatish-sayyi'ah). 'Good' (hasanah) refers to patience, character, and forgiveness, and 'evil' (sayyi'ah) refers to the insults and accusations of their opponents. When they embraced Islam, they were labeled as traitors by their nation, but they responded to every insult with dignity, character, and forgiveness.

'And spend from what We have provided them' (Wa mimma razaqnahum yunfiqoon) has a specific context. The Qur'an highlights that the primary barrier for the Jews in accepting Islam was their love for wealth. In this Surah too, the story of Karun (Korah) is mentioned ahead, and other Surahs discuss their obsession with wealth. Those who are possessed by the love of wealth can never accept the truth. Lord Jesus said: 'It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.' Surah al-Lahab shows that Abu Lahab's hostility was also rooted in his love for wealth. By referencing 'and spend from what We have provided them,' the Qur'an highlights that these righteous people were free from the love of wealth, which is why they were blessed with the guidance of Islam.

Turning away from the ignorant:
This further explains 'they ward off evil with good': when they hear vain talk from their opponents, they turn away from them, saying: 'For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds; let us follow our path, and you follow yours.'

'Peace be upon you' (Salamun 'alaykum) is a parting greeting, identical to the greeting Abraham gave to his father. It is a highly dignified way of parting from others. 'We seek not the ignorant' (la nabtaghil-jahileen) describes their attitude: they do not wish to engage in debates with the ignorant, and part from them with a greeting.

Consolation for the Prophet:
Guidance here means making someone guided. This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): do not exhaust yourself over them. Your duty is only to convey the message; it is not in your power to make anyone guided. Rather, guidance is in the hands of Allah, who guides whom He wills, and He knows best who is worthy of guidance. We have explained the divine law regarding guidance at various places.

A standard satanic tactic against the divine system:
This quotes another objection of the Quraysh: 'If we follow the guidance with you, we will be swept from our land.'

Since the Quraysh enjoyed peace and political dominance throughout the country, they attributed this to their religion of polytheism. They believed their idols were the source of their prosperity, and had gathered the idols of all tribes in the Kaaba to maintain their religious and political influence over them. When the Prophet presented the call of monotheism, they warned their people that if they abandoned their idols and worshipped only one God, their unity would be shattered, all Arab tribes would turn against them, and they would be expelled from their land. This is identical to the objection modern leaders raise against the implementation of the Islamic system, arguing that it would disrupt our economy and isolate us from the world. This is also identical to the objection Pharaoh and his chiefs raised against Moses: 'He wants to destroy your exemplary way.' This has always been the primary satanic tactic against the divine system.

The blessings of the Kaaba:
'Have We not established for them a secure sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things?' (A-wa-lam numakkin-lahum haraman aminay-yujba ilayhi thamaratu kulli shay'). When Abraham settled his family in this barren valley, he prayed for their security and sustenance. Consequently, the sanctuary became so blessed that products from all regions are brought to it. We have discussed the blessings of the Kaaba in previous Surahs. The text states: 'They received these blessings due to the grace of Allah and the prayer of Abraham, yet these ungrateful people think that if they follow the religion of Abraham, they will be destroyed!'

'But most of them do not know' (Wa lakinna aktharahum la ya'lamoon) is an expression of sorrow over their ignorance of their own history.

In Surah al-Ankabut:

'Have they not seen that We made [Makkah] a safe sanctuary, while people are swept away all around them? Then in falsehood do they believe, and in the favor of Allah do they disbelieve?' (al-Ankabut: 67)

'Fruits' (thamarāt) was explained under Surah al-Baqarah: it refers not only to fruits, but to all food grains and provisions.

'Numakkin-lahum haraman aminan' is identical in style to 'And We will surely establish for them their religion' (an-Noor: 55). History testifies that Allah has made this sanctuary secure by its own sanctity; it does not require others for its protection, but protects others. We will discuss this in detail in Surah Quraysh.

A linguistic feature of Arabic:
'Batar' means to be arrogant, boastful, and ungrateful. Since it is not used transitively, there are various opinions regarding the accusative case of 'ma'eeshataha'. Some assume an omitted preposition, while others treat it as an adverb. The Ustadh-e-Imam was of the view that it is the direct object of 'ahlakna'. However, none of these opinions seem fully satisfactory. Some have suggested that 'batirat' carries the meaning of 'kafarat' (was ungrateful), which is closest to the truth. In Arabic, 'batiral-haqq' is used to mean 'he arrogantly rejected the truth,' and 'batiral-la'n' means 'he held the blessing in contempt and was ungrateful for it.' In our view, this is based on the principle of inclusion, and is used as such in the verse.

The end of ungrateful nations:
This is a warning to the Quraysh: 'You think your security and prosperity are due to your own administrative skills, and feel proud of it, whereas all this is due to the grace of Allah. You should show gratitude instead of pride, for many ungrateful nations before you were destroyed because of their pride, and their ruined dwellings are not far from you; you pass by them during your travels.'

'These' (Tilka) shows that these ruins were familiar to the Quraysh, referring to Thamud and Lot's people whose dwellings lay along their trade routes. 'They have not been inhabited after them except a little' carries the same meaning as Surah Hud: 'That is from the news of the cities which We relate to you; of them, some are standing and some are wiped out' (Hud: 100), as explained there. It can also mean that their architectural majesty was completely destroyed, and only nomadic shepherds occasionally stop there.

'And We were the heirs' (Wa kunna Nahnul-waritheen) means those who claimed ownership of these lands have vanished, and their inheritance returned to Us, for the true Owner and Heir of the heavens and the earth is Allah alone.

Destruction occurs only after the commission of a messenger:
This further explains the divine law regarding the destruction of nations: 'And your Lord would never destroy the cities until He had sent to their mother city a messenger reciting to them Our verses.' This means that the first phase of this divine law has been completed: Allah has raised His Messenger in their mother city—Makkah—who is reciting His verses to them day and night. Now, the descent of punishment depends on their conduct.

'And We would not destroy the cities unless its people were wrongdoers' (Wa ma kunna muhlikil-qura illa wa ahluha zalimoon) describes the second phase: 'We never destroy a nation unless they wrong themselves by rejecting the messenger's warnings.' The meaning is that the Quraysh have reached this critical stage; if they reject the Messenger, they will face the promised punishment after a specified respite, and this will be absolute justice.

Direct warning to the Quraysh:
The previous warnings were indirect, and now the chiefs of the Quraysh are addressed directly: 'Do not boast of your worldly prosperity; whatever you have been given is but the provision of this life and its temporary decoration. The true, lasting reward is with Allah, which will be granted to those who seek the afterlife and strive for it. Do not destroy your eternal life for this temporary decoration; use your common sense!'

'Ihdar' means to bring forward in custody like criminals; thus, the word 'muhdaroon' carries the sense of being brought forward in humiliation.

Meaning: 'Consider the difference between two people: one whom We have promised a great reward in the afterlife—which he is sure to receive, for none is more true to His promise than Allah—and another whom We have allowed to enjoy the temporary provisions of this life, but who will be brought forward in humiliation on the Day of Judgment. Which of the two is better and has an everlasting home?'

The upcoming subject... Verses 62-75
Now, to reinforce the previous discourse, the falsity of polytheism and the helplessness of false deities are discussed: if anyone has assumed that their idols will benefit or intercede for them on the Day of Judgment, they should dismiss this illusion. These false deities have no share in the administration of this world, nor can they help in the hereafter. Those who are rejecting the Qur'an based on reliance on these idols should consider their end. Read the verses in this light.

ویوم بنادیھم فیقول این شرکآءی الذین کنتم تزعمون 62
Resurrection and the end of the polytheists:
This and the upcoming verses are a series of warnings describing the gathering of the polytheists, their leaders, and their idols on the Day of Judgment. The text states: 'Do not forget the Day when Allah will summon them, asking: "Where are My partners whom you used to claim?"' The tone of 'Where are My partners' carries a powerful expression of His wrath, implying: 'Produce them now, so that you and they may face your end together.'

The dispute between the leaders and their followers:
'Those against whom the word has come true' (Haqqa 'alayhi-mul-qawl) refers to the leaders of disbelief against whom the decree of punishment has been issued. The followers will point to them: 'Our Lord, these are the ones who led us astray; had they not led us astray, we would have been guided.' Their leaders will immediately respond: 'We were deviated ourselves, and we led them along; they chose to follow us of their own accord. We declare our dissociation from them before You; they did not worship us.' Meaning, they did not worship us; rather, they followed their own desires. This dispute between the leaders and their followers is recorded at various places in the Qur'an. For example, in Surah al-A'raf:

'He will say: "Enter among communities which had passed away before you of jinn and mankind into the Fire." Every time a community enters, it will curse its sister until, when they have all overtaken one another therein, the last of them will say about the first, "Our Lord, these had misled us, so give them a double punishment of the Fire." He will say: "For each is double, but you do not know." And the first of them will say to the last of them, "Then you had not over us any favor, so taste the punishment for what you used to earn."' (al-A'raf: 38-39)

We will explain this further in Surah Fussilat, verse 47.

The helplessness of false deities:
Meaning, when their leaders declare their dissociation, the polytheists will be commanded: 'Call your partners.' They will call out to their idols—such as Lat, Manat, Uzza, and Na'ilah—but the idols will not respond, as they were mere figments of their imagination. On that Day, the reality will be exposed that they worshipped mere illusions. It should be noted that this refers to the Arab polytheists whose idols were completely imaginary; those who worshipped actual figures—such as the Christians who worshipped Lord Jesus—are discussed separate, showing that those righteous figures will declare their innocence before Allah, stating they had only called people to the worship of Allah.

'And they will see the punishment' (Wa ra'awul-adhab) means the idols they relied upon will vanish, and the punishment of Allah will be right before them.

'If only they had been guided' (law annahum kanoo yahtadoon) is an expression of sorrow over their unmindfulness: 'If only they had accepted guidance, they would have been saved from this punishment.'

The questioning of the polytheists on the Day of Judgment:
This is another detail of the Day of Judgment: they will be summoned and asked: 'What response did you give to the messengers?' This question is intended to highlight the gravity of their crime, as if to say: 'Allah sent His messengers to guide you and establish proof, so what did you do with His guidance?'

'Amiyat 'alayhimul-anba' is identical in meaning to being completely stunned and speechless. It means such panic and confusion will dominate them that they will find no words to speak.

'And they will not ask one another' (fahum la yatasa'aloon) means they will be so bewildered that they will not even be able to ask one another for help. In times of panic, followers look to their leaders, but the leaders will be concerned only with themselves, as explained above. This has passed in Surah al-Ma'idah, verse 109.

Glad tidings for the believers:
When 'perhaps' ('asa) is used in relation to Allah, it carries the meaning of a promise and glad tidings.

After describing the end of the ungrateful, glad tidings are given to those who repent from polytheism, believe, and do righteous deeds: 'Perhaps they will be among the successful.' While they should not feel entirely secure—since salvation is by His grace—they have every right to hope, for He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

The true status of angels:
Based on context, 'what He wills' is omitted after 'chooses' (yakhtaru). This refers to the angels: they are creatures of Allah, and if they have been granted closeness and honor, it is by His grace. 'Allah chooses from the angels messengers and from the people' (al-Hajj: 75). Meaning, any honor or closeness the angels possess is a gift from Him; it does not give them any share in His divinity. 'They have no choice' (ma kana lahumul-khiyarah) means they possess no independent authority. 'Exalted is Allah and high above what they associate with Him' (Subhanallahi wa ta'ala 'amma yushrikoon). Allah is free from the partners they associate with Him, for associating partners with Him is contrary to His absolute attributes. Since the partners worshipped by the Arab polytheists (verse 64) were assumed to be angels, the text clarifies their true status: they have some honor among His creation, but it is a gift from Him, and they possess no independent authority.

وربک یعلم ماتکن صدورھم ومایعلنون 69
Refuting polytheism through His all-encompassing knowledge:
This refutes polytheism through His all-encompassing knowledge: 'And your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they declare.' Since He is fully aware of all secrets and manifest deeds, why would He require any partner in His kingdom? A ruler requires partners only when he is dependent on others for information, and intercession is required only when someone can add to His knowledge. Since He is All-Knowing, on what basis do they worship the angels in hope of their intercession!

Summary:
This summarizes the previous discourse: 'He is Allah; there is no deity except Him.' Any other deity worshipped besides Him is false. The Arabs acknowledged Him as the supreme God, but associated partners with Him; this verse refutes their polytheism.

'To Him is praise in the first and the Hereafter' (lahul-hamdu fil-oola wal-akhirah). All blessings in this world and the hereafter are from Him alone; therefore, He alone deserves praise and gratitude in both worlds. The praise of the believers in Paradise after their success has passed in Surah Yunus.

'And to Him belongs the decision, and to Him you will be returned' (wa lahul-hukmu wa ilayhi turja'oon). The final decision is His, and everyone must return to Him; there is no other refuge.

Verses 71-73
Arguing from their own admissions:
'Say' (Qul) here is used in the sense of asking a rhetorical question whose answer is obvious to the opponent. Since the Arab polytheists acknowledged Allah as the sole Creator of the heavens, earth, night, and day, the text asks them: 'Say: "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the night continuous until the Day of Resurrection, which deity other than Allah could bring you light?"' Similarly: 'Say: "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the day continuous until the Day of Resurrection, which deity other than Allah could bring you night in which you rest?"' Meaning, since your false deities have no control over these essential phenomena, on what basis have they been made partners in His divinity?

The eloquence of the style:
It is noteworthy that after mentioning the continuous night, the text states: 'Will you not hear?' (a-fa-la tasma'oon), and after the continuous day: 'Will you not see?' (a-fa-la tubsiroon). This is a highly visual style: the first question is asked, as it were, in the dark of night, implying: 'If you cannot see in the dark, can you not even hear? Why do you not listen to this truth?' The second is asked, as it were, in the bright of day, implying: 'In this bright light, can you not even see this obvious truth?'

This also treats abstract realities as physical perceptions, showing that these are obvious truths requiring only open eyes and ears. Yet, you understand nothing. 

Another detail is that while the utility of the night ('in which you rest') is mentioned, the utility of the day is omitted in that verse. This is in accordance with the rule of omission for conciseness, and is clarified in the subsequent verse.

Gratitude for His blessings:
'And out of His mercy He made for you the night and the day' (Wa min-rahmatihi ja'ala lakumul-layla wan-nahara). The alternation of night and day is entirely out of His mercy; none has any claim on Him to provide this comfort. He did so: 'that you may rest therein' (referring to the night) 'and that you may seek of His bounty' (referring to the day), 'and that you may be grateful.' Seeking His bounty refers to human effort for sustenance, which should conform to His law. Utilizing His resources contrary to His law is a form of rebellion.

Verses 74-75
The final establishment of proof:
Verse 74 is identical to the one that began this discourse, and serves here to introduce another detail. On the Day of Judgment, Allah will establish proof against the polytheists through His messengers, who will testify that they had called people to monotheism. Subsequently, the polytheists will be commanded: 'Produce your proof.' At that moment, they will find no argument, and all their false deities will vanish.

'A witness' (shaheed) refers to the messengers sent to every nation. They are described as witnesses because they testified to the truth in this world and will testify in the hereafter that they had conveyed the message. While the polytheists claim their practices are based on the teachings of their prophets, the messengers' testimony will refute this claim, leaving them speechless. This has been explained in Surah al-Ma'idah, verses 116-117.

The Ustadh-e-Imam interpreted this verse in the light of Surah Maryam, verse 69, and Surah Fussilat, verse 47. However, I prefer the explanation presented above, which is supported by other verses of the Qur'an.

The upcoming subject... Verses 76-84
Next, the discourse connects with verse 58. Previously, it was stated that many ungrateful nations were destroyed despite their prosperity, and their idols could not save them. Following a parenthetical refutation of polytheism, the text returns to the central theme, presenting the story of Karun (Korah)—a wealthy Israelite who grew arrogant because of his wealth and met a disastrous end. This serves as a warning to those who are obsessed with the transient decorations of this life. The counterpart to Karun among the Quraysh was Abu Lahab, whose wealth was a source of trial for himself and others. Relating this story is intended to open their eyes. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

Karun's envy of Moses:
The story of Karun (Qorub in the Torah) is recorded in the Book of Numbers, chapter 16. His lineage shows that he was from the tribe of Levi, and was a cousin of Moses and Aaron. He envied their religious and political leadership, and rallied support against them, arguing: 'All members of the community are holy; why should Moses and Aaron exalt themselves over the congregation?'
