He allied with some nobles and initiated a rebellion against Moses. The Torah describes this as follows:

'Korah son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, and certain Reubenites—Dathan and Abiram, sons of Eliab, and On son of Peleth—became insolent and rose up against Moses. With them were 250 Israelite men, well-known community leaders who had been appointed members of the council. They came as a group to oppose Moses and Aaron and said to them, "You have gone too far! The whole community is holy, every one of them, and the Lord is with them. Why then do you set yourselves above the Lord’s assembly?"' (Numbers 16:1-3)

Karun's massive wealth:
The Qur'an describes his rebellion as: 'And he tyrannized over them' (fa-bagha 'alayhim).

'And We gave him of treasures such that its keys would burden a band of strong men' (Wa aataynahu minal-kunoozi ma inna mafatihahu la-tanoo'u bil-'usbati olil-quwwah). This describes his massive wealth to show the cause of his pride and rebellion: since he was highly wealthy, he refused to accept the leadership of Moses and Aaron. The description of his keys is not a mere hyperbole, but a statement of fact. In ancient times, they did not have the secure safes and locks we have today. Wealthy individuals constructed underground treasuries and secured their massive doors with heavy iron bars and locks, which were difficult to carry or turn. Carrying these heavy locks and keys required a team of strong men. This was also the practice for securing the gates of fortresses and walled cities. The text points to such heavy keys. Having such keys was a symbol of extreme wealth.

His people's advice to Karun:
'When his people said to him: "Do not exult..."' (Idh qala lahu qawmuhu...). When he grew arrogant, the wise among his people advised him not to boast of his wealth, reminding him that Allah does not love the boastful. This was a gentle warning that pride leads to divine punishment. While 'his people' is a general term, context indicates it refers to the wise among them, as clarified in verse 80.

This further details the advice given to Karun, where every phrase is a treasure of wisdom.

'But seek, through that which Allah has given you, the home of the Hereafter' (Wabtaghi fee ma aatakallahu-Daral-Akhirah). This means your wealth was not given to you for pride, but to seek the pleasure of Allah and the reward of the hereafter. It is a gift from Him to test your conduct: whether you turn it into an idol or utilize it for His cause.

'And do not forget your share of the world' (Wa la tansa naseebaka minad-dunya). This is a highly wise reminder: remember that everything in this world is temporary; the only portion that will benefit you in the eternal life is what you spend in His cause. If you hoard gold and silver and ignore the rights of God and His servants, this wealth will turn into a source of ruin.

Most commentators have interpreted this as: 'Do not ignore your worldly pleasures; do not abandon the world for the hereafter.' In our view, this is incorrect, and the true meaning has been clarified above.

'And do good as Allah has done good to you' (Wa ahsin kama ahsanallahu ilayka). Show compassion to others just as Allah has shown grace to you, for He wants His servants to display His qualities of compassion and generosity.

Meaning of corruption on earth:
'And desire not corruption in the land. Indeed, Allah does not love the corrupters' (Wa la tabghil-fasada fil-ard innal-laha la yuhibbul-mufsideen). Corruption on earth is not limited to theft and robbery; its primary form is self-exaltation and rebellion (uluww). When a person transgresses the limits of servitude and claims independent authority, he is a corrupter. Allah does not love such people, and will ultimately uproot them.

Karun's response:
Karun replied: 'I was only given it because of knowledge I have' (Innama ooteetuhu 'ala 'ilmin 'indee). He claimed his wealth was due to his own skills and administrative expertise, and had nothing to do with God's grace. His words 'knowledge I have' ( 'ilmin 'indee) reveal his pride, as if he were independent of God's help. He dismissed their advice completely.

Allah's comment:
This is Allah's comment on his pride: 'Did he not know that Allah had destroyed before him of generations those who were greater than him in power and greater in accumulation?' He forgot that the contemporary Pharaoh was drowned shortly before this. 'Power' refers to military strength, and 'accumulation' refers to wealth and followers.

'And the criminals will not be asked about their sins' (Wa la yus'alu 'an dhunoobihimul-mujrimoon) means when the decree of punishment is issued, they are granted no time to offer excuses; rather, the punishment strikes them suddenly, and on the Day of Judgment, their limbs will testify against them without any need for investigation: 'The criminals will be known by their marks, and they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet' (ar-Rahman: 41).

Karun's parade and its effect on the public:
It appears Karun organized a grand parade to display his wealth and followers, trying to intimidate Moses (peace be upon him) and attract more supporters. 'In his adornment' (fee zeenatihi) indicates he displayed his wealth in an elaborate manner, which always impresses the simple-minded. This is identical to the public demonstrations organized by political leaders today. Its effect on those who seek only the world was that they exclaimed: 'Oh, would that we had the like of what Karun was given! Indeed, he is one of great fortune.'

The stance of the wise:
The wise among the people advised them: 'Woe to you! The reward of Allah is better for him who believes and does righteousness.' The true value lies in faith and righteous deeds, not in temporary wealth, but this wisdom is granted only to those who remain steadfast against worldly desires.

True knowledge is the recognition of God and the afterlife:
'Those who were given knowledge' (Ootul-'ilm) refers to those who possess true knowledge—recognition of God and the afterlife. This is the true light, without which a person remains in darkness, regardless of his scientific or financial expertise. Karun was highly skilled in financial matters, but was devoid of true knowledge; thus, his wealth turned into a source of ruin. This applies to all fields of knowledge: they benefit humans only when accompanied by true knowledge; otherwise, they make a person clever and cunning, but not human, which is highly dangerous.

Wisdom is granted to the steadfast:
'And none is granted it except the patient' (Wa la yulaqqaha illas-sabiroon). The pronoun refers to the wisdom and advice they offered. This shows that true knowledge and patience are linked, and true wisdom is granted only to those who are steadfast, willing to sacrifice worldly wealth for the sake of the hereafter.

It is possible that this phrase is a comment from Allah praising the wise, implying: 'They were blessed with this wisdom because they remained steadfast and were not influenced by Karun's wealth.'

Karun's end:
Some details of the narrative are omitted here. The Torah records that when Moses (peace be upon him) saw the rebellion of Karun and his allies, he called them to a challenge (mubahalah) to manifest who was accepted by God. The Torah describes this as follows:

'Dathan and Abiram came out and stood with their wives, children and little ones at the entrances to their tents. Then Moses said, "This is how you will know that the Lord has sent me to do all these things and that it was not my own idea: If these men die a natural death and suffer only what commonly befalls mankind, then the Lord has not sent me. But if the Lord brings about something totally new, and the earth opens its mouth and swallows them, with everything that belongs to them, and they go down alive into the realm of the dead, then you will know that these men have treated the Lord with contempt." As soon as he finished saying all this, the ground under them split apart and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them and their households, and all those associated with Korah, together with their possessions. They went down alive into the realm of the dead, with everything they owned; the earth closed over them, and they perished and were gone from the community. At their cries, all the Israelites around them fled, shouting, "The earth is going to swallow us too!"' (Numbers 16:27-34)

This shows that the ground swallowed them in response to Moses' invocation, and Karun could neither defend himself nor could his hosts save him.

The regret of the public:
Simple-minded people who envy the wealthy and think their success is permanent realize the truth only when the wealthy are destroyed. Then they recall the advice of the wise, declaring: 'Indeed, Allah extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts it.' Those who had envied Karun the previous day now exclaimed: 'Had Allah not favored us, He would have caused the earth to swallow us as well. Ah, how the disbelievers do not succeed!'

Summary of the lesson:
This summarizes the lesson: 'That home of the Hereafter We assign to those who do not desire exaltedness upon the earth or corruption, and the [best] outcome is for the righteous.' The eternal home is reserved for those who are free from pride (uluww) and corruption, and are righteous.

The law of recompense:
This defines the law of recompense: 'Whoever comes with a good deed will have better than it' (out of His grace), 'and whoever comes with an evil deed—then those who did evil deeds will not be recompensed except for what they used to do' (out of His justice).

The upcoming subject... Verses 85-88
Next are the concluding verses of the Surah. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: you did not demand this Qur'an from God; rather, He chose to reveal it to you and He will assist you in fulfilling its duties, leading you to ultimate success. Therefore, do not worry about the opponents; remain firm on your call, and let no opposition weaken your stance, for Allah is with you. Read the verses in this light.

Bsharat of success for the Prophet:
'Ma'ad' means the destination, goal, or ultimate home. Its indefinite form denotes grandeur, referring to an honorable and grand destination.

Following the discourse, the Prophet is consoled: 'Do not worry about the opponents. The One who imposed upon you the Qur'an will surely take you back to an honorable destination, alienating your enemies. This is His definitive decree, and His law regarding His messengers is unchangeable.'

'Say: "My Lord is most knowing of who brings guidance and who is in clear error"' means: 'Tell your opponents that Allah knows who is guided and who is in clear error; the truth has been presented, and the final judgment rests with Him.' The 'guidance' (al-huda) refers to His divine guidance, and rejecting it is indeed 'clear error.'

Allah assists His servants in the duties He imposes upon them:
This further explains the previous verse: 'You did not expect that the Book would be revealed to you; rather, it is a mercy from your Lord.' Since He chose to reveal it to you, He will guide you in fulfilling its duties, and since it is a mercy, its ultimate end will be a source of mercy for you and those who accept it. Those who reject it are only depriving themselves of His mercy.

Warning to the Quraysh:
'So never be an assistant to the disbelievers' (Fa-la takoonanna zaheerallil-kafireen). Although addressed to the Prophet, the warning is directed at the deniers: 'Convey this revelation without any compromise. If they try to pressure you, do not show any compliance or compromise, for you are accountable to Allah, not to them. Showing compromise would make you an assistant to the wrongdoers.' This was to make the chiefs of the Quraysh completely despair of ever forcing the Prophet to compromise.

First, this shows that prophethood is a divine gift, not an acquisition through human effort.

Second, if a duty is imposed upon a person without his personal desire, Allah assists him in fulfilling it. This is also supported by various Hadiths.

Third, showing compromise in the matters of the Qur'an makes a person an assistant to the wrongdoers.

Emphasizing what the polytheists hated most:
This further emphasizes the previous verse: 'And never let them avert you from the verses of Allah after they have been revealed to you. Invite to your Lord, and never be of those who associate partners with Him.' Convey His verses fearlessly, for His word cannot be suppressed, and the responsibility of conveying it rests on you. 'Invite to your Lord, and never be of those who associate partners with Him' targets what the Quraysh hated most—monotheism—warning that any compromise in this regard would make you one of the polytheists.

Eliminating all possibilities of compromise:
This final verse eliminates all possibilities of compromise: 'And do not invoke with Allah another deity. There is no deity except Him. Everything will be destroyed except His Face. His is the judgment, and to Him you will be returned.' He is the sole God; everything is temporary except His Essence, the final decision is His, and everyone must return to Him.
